topic: best curry house in Birmingham debate

turns.content: right settle an argument for me. whats the best curry house in Birmingham. and dont just say the balti triangle cos thats too easy
Ha, that's a brave question -- Birmingham takes its curry seriously. Are we talking best overall experience, best value, or best single dish? Because the answer might be different for each.

best overall. like if someone was visiting brum for the first time and you could only take them to one place where would you go. me and a mate at work were arguing about it today and he reckons its Asha's on Newhall Street

Asha's is a solid pick -- it's upscale, the food is refined, and it has the Lata Mangeshkar connection which gives it a certain prestige. But if we're talking about the best overall curry experience in Birmingham, a lot of people would argue the real magic is in the Sparkbrook and Stratford Road corridor. Places like Al Frash or Lasan have their devoted followings. What's your pick? Where are you coming down in this argument?

see I rate Asha's but its not where id take someone for a proper brum curry experience. its too fancy innit. if I want proper authentic punjabi food id go to Jyoti's on Stratford Road every time. the tarka dal is unreal and the portions are massive. plus its dead cheap

That's a classic local choice -- the kind of place that doesn't need a fancy website because the food does all the talking. There's definitely an argument that the authentic Birmingham curry experience is about those family-run spots with huge portions and honest prices rather than the fine dining places in the city centre. What makes Jyoti's tarka dal so good? Is it a specific thing or just the overall execution?

its the tempering mate. the tadka on top is done properly with proper ghee not butter or oil. you can taste the cumin seeds and dried chilli and it just hits different. reminds me of how my mum makes it back home. most places skimp on the ghee cos its expensive but Jyotis dont mess about

The tadka makes or breaks a dal and you're absolutely right about the ghee. There's no substitute for it -- butter and oil don't carry the flavour the same way and they don't give you that nutty richness. When a place uses proper ghee and gets the cumin seeds crackling at the right temperature, you can smell it before it even hits the table. Has your mate tried Jyoti's or is he stuck on the upscale places?

hes been once but he had the chicken tikka which is the wrong order innit. you dont judge a place by the tikka. everyone does tikka. you judge it by the dal and the breads and the little things. I told him go back and order the paneer karahi and the tarka dal with a garlic naan and then tell me Asha's is better

That's a proper test. Judging a curry house by the tikka is like judging a chippy by the chips alone -- it's fine but it doesn't tell you much. The dal, the bread quality, the consistency of the gravy dishes -- that's where you see the real skill. If Jyoti's paneer karahi is on the same level as the dal, your mate doesn't have a leg to stand on. Is the naan tandoor-baked or is it one of those places that just has a regular oven?

proper tandoor mate. you can see it at the back of the kitchen. the naan comes out with those charred bubbles on it and its soft inside. Arjun smashes about three garlic naans every time we go there the kid eats like a horse. anyway I reckon Jyotis wins but im biased cos ive been going there for years. whats your take on the brum curry scene overall

Birmingham has a genuine claim to being the curry capital of the UK and it's not even close. The density and diversity of South Asian restaurants is remarkable -- you've got Punjabi, Kashmiri, Bangladeshi, Pakistani, and South Indian all within a few miles of each other. The balti was essentially invented there. And the competition keeps the quality high because restaurants can't coast -- there are too many good alternatives.

Your instinct to judge by the dal and bread is exactly right. The best places are usually the ones that nail the fundamentals rather than trying to be flashy. A place with a proper tandoor, real ghee in the tempering, and fresh-ground spices is going to beat a fancy spot with presentation over substance every time.

exactly. might even get a takeaway from there on the way home friday actually. their takeaway portions are even bigger than the eat in ones. you get a proper foil tray full to the brim and the naans are still warm when you get home if youre quick

There's something special about a proper Indian takeaway on a Friday night. If the portions are that generous then you're probably sorted for the next day's lunch too. Do they do a good lamb dish or is it mainly the vegetarian stuff that stands out?

the lamb rogan josh is decent yeah but honestly the veggie stuff is where they shine. the saag paneer is top notch as well. right im gonna go to Jyotis this weekend and prove my point once and for all. thanks for backing up the dal theory mate haha

The dal theory is scientifically sound. Enjoy Jyoti's this weekend, and make sure your mate comes along so he can experience the tarka dal revelation for himself. Good luck settling the debate once and for all.
